title: The Salt Inspector
description: Munshi Vanshidar lacks the ability to be dishonest or corrupt in spite of being surrounded by greedy people soaked in money. As the salt inspector, he is true to himself and to the integrity of his work. Soon, this leads him to clash with Pandit Alopideen, the most powerful man in their area. What follows is a tale that will always be relevant to our society, portraying the constant war between the lure of money and the need to be moral.

pages.text:
About the Author
Born on the 31st of July in 1880 as Dhanpat Rai Srivastava, Premchand went on to become one of the most prolific writers in the Indian subcontinent. He was bestowed with the honorary title of ‘Munshi’ due to his mastery over words. He is considered to be the pioneer of the modernist Hindustani literature. He authored (roughly) 300 short stories and several dozen novels in his lifetime. Premchand was also a popular dramatist and a translator. His famous works include novels like Karmabhoomi, Seva Sadan, Godaan, Nirmala.... and short stories like Eidgah, Lottery, Bade Bhai Sahab, Do Bailon ki Katha and Poos Ki Raat amongst many other notable titles.. He was given the title of ‘“Upanyas Samrat’” (Emperor among Novelists) by the other writers of his times.Majority of his writings present a slice of life of the pre-independence era. His writing is steeped in realism and he is known for his Hindi-Urdu literature. His style of writing is more direct and he is considered to be the first Hindi writer to showcase the concept of realism in his writings. Subjects like colonialism, corruption, poverty, child widowhood and exploitation of the poor etc. feature prominently in his works. He wrote extensively about the life of common people of his times. Munshi Premchand died on October 8, 1936 but his work continues to be commemorated even today.

About the Story
The Salt Inspector Munshi* Vanshidhar is an honest officer. Despite his father’s counsel to the contrary and the powerful Pandit Alopideen’s offer of a huge sum of money, he prefers to stick to his duty. The reward for his honesty is a reprimand from the court and suspension from his job. This is nothing short of a disaster for his family.
Though the court favours him, Pandit Alopideen, upon realising the long term benefits of having an unshakably honest man in his team, arrives at Munshi Vanshidhar’s humble home, praises him for following his duty and persuades him to join as the administrator of his considerable estate.

When the new Salt Department was formed and a ban was imposed on using something as simple and God-given as salt; people began to trade in it surreptitiously. Many cons and tricks were ushered in; some would bribe their way around, some would use sheer cunning and trickery. The authorities were making hay while the sun shone. People were leaving the widely-honoured post of patwari* (officer-in-charge of Land Records) and fawning over the Salt Department. Even the lawyers eyed the post of Salt Inspector.

This was a time when English education was suspected to lead one to Christianity. Persian was the dominant language, the language of administration. Having studied love stories and odes to passion and love, a person well-versed in Persian would get appointed to high office.
After wading through Zuleikha’s* anguish over her separation from her lover, Munshi Vanshidhar too set out in search of a job. He considered the love story of Shirin and Farhad* far more important than the battle of Nal and Neel*, and the discovery of America.

Munshi Vanshidhar’s father was much experienced in the ways of the world. He counselled, “Son! You understand our plight. We are under heavy debt. The girls are growing fast as weeds, and I am like an old tree at the bank of the river. I might keel over anytime. Now you are the master of the household, the head of this family.
When you accept a job, don’t pay any attention to the designation. A job is like a pir’s mazar* where one has to keep one’s sight trained on the offerings. Look for a job which offers, apart from the monthly salary, scope for additional income. Remember, a salary is like the full moon, it is seen only for a day and then it wanes. The additional income is like a flowing river that will always quench your thirst. A salary comes through human agency, it has its limits. The ‘additional income’ is God’s gift, it will lead to prosperity.

You are wise my son, no need for me to say more.
You must be discreet. Gauge the man, gauge his need and check if the time is opportune. Then do what you deem fit. Being tough with a man who needs a favour from you results in profit. But to bring under pressure a man who doesn’t need any favours from you is somewhat difficult. This is the essence of my life’s experience.”
After this sermon his father blessed him. Ever the obedient son, Vanshidhar listened to his father in silence. He considered patience his friend, wisdom his guide, and self-reliance his support in the vast world.

It must have been an auspicious moment when Vanshidhar stepped out of his home, because soon he was appointed the Salt Inspector at the Salt Department. The salary was good and the opportunities to earn additional income were endless. When his old father received the news of his son’s appointment, he was overcome with joy. The moneylenders’ behaviour softened, the wine-trader’s hope blossomed afresh. The neighbours got heartaches.
It was a winter night. The constables and guards of the Salt Department were drunk. Munshi Vanshidhar had joined the department less than six months ago but within that short span his efficiency and fine conduct had impressed his officers. He had won their trust.

The river Yamuna flowed a mile to the east of the Salt Department office. It had a pontoon-bridge over it. The inspector was cocooned in sweet sleep. Suddenly, he woke up, instead of the sound of the flowing river; he heard the rumble of carts and shouts of boatmen.
He wondered why the carts were crossing the river so late at night, and suspected there was something fishy going on. His logic strengthened his suspicion. He pulled on his uniform, placed the pistol in his pocket and rode to the bridge. A long line of carts was crossing the bridge. “Whose carts are these?” He asked angrily.

The men whispered among themselves then one of them replied, “Pandit Alopideen’s.”
“Who is Pandit Alopideen?”
“The one from Dataganj.”
Munshi Vanshidhar was taken aback. Pandit Alopideen was the most distinguished landlord of the area. A big trader and moneylender, he dealt in huge sums of money. There wasn't a trader, big or small, who wasn’t indebted to him. His business was vast and he was a wily man. He was the constant host to British officers who came to hunt in the region.

The Munshi asked, “Where are the carts headed to?”
“Kanpur.”
But when he inquired what was loaded in them, his question met with silence.
The Salt Inspector’s suspicion grew. He waited for an answer then raised his voice, “Are you all mute?
I asked, what are the carts loaded with?”
When he did not get an answer this time either, he checked the sacks. His suspicion was confirmed. The sacks contained lumps of salt.

Pandit Alopideen was half asleep in his handsome wagon. Some anxious cart-drivers woke him up, “Maharaj*, the Salt Inspector has halted the carts. He has summoned you to the river bank.”
Pandit Alopideen had an unshakable faith in Goddess Lakshmi*. He used to say, “Leave alone the world, even heaven is ruled by Goddess Lakshmi.” His observation reflected the reality. After all, justice and morality are just puppets in her hands, she makes them dance to her whims. Reclining on the mattress he replied imperiously, “I am coming.”

Pandit Alopideen leisurely rolled a paan*, then wrapping a blanket around himself, approached the Salt Inspector, “I bless you Sir*. Please tell me what crime I have committed so these carts have been halted? We Brahmins ought to be under your benevolent grace.”
“Government orders,” Munshi Vanshidhar replied gruffly.

Pandit Alopideen laughed, “I don’t know any Government orders. For me, you are the Government. It is a matter between the two of us. Could I ever ignore you? You need not have troubled yourself. It is impossible for me to go this way and not make offerings to the presiding deity of this riverbank. I was preparing to come to pay obeisance to you.” The words of the rich man failed to mellow Munshi Vanshidhar. He was drunk on honesty.

He spoke sternly, “I am not one of those traitors who sell their souls for a few pennies. For now, you are under arrest. You will be fined according to the rules. Jamadaar* Badlu Singh, I order you to arrest him.”
Pandit Alopideen was stunned. There was a commotion among the cart drivers. This was perhaps the first time Panditjee was hearing such harsh words. Badlu Singh stepped forward but couldn’t muster the courage to touch Pandit Alopideen.

Panditjee had never seen dharma* (morality) slight dhan* (money). He thought the boy was being insolent, was ignorant of the power of riches; perhaps he was callow. He beseeched, “Saheb, please don’t arrest me. I will be ruined. My reputation will be besmirched.
What would you gain by insulting me? I cannot dream of going against your wishes.”
The Munshi replied sternly, “I do not wish to listen to such talk.”

The support which Alopideen had thought to be a strong rock seemed to be shifting from under his feet. His pride and affluence took a hard blow. Yet, his faith in the power of money remained intact. He ordered his agent, “Lalaji, please offer a thousand rupees to Babu Saheb*. He is like a hungry lion at the moment.”
Munshi Vanshidhar lost his temper, “Not even a lakh rupees will dissuade me from the path of truth!”

Pandit Alopideen’s worldliness was much frustrated at this mindless stubbornness of morality accompanied by rare sacrifice. A conflict ensued between the two powers. Money made aggressive attacks, the offer went from one thousand to five thousand, ten to fifteen, and even as high as twenty thousand. With divine courage, morality stood its ground.
Alopideen gave up, “I cannot offer more. Now, it’s up to you.”

Munshi Vanshidhar loudly called for the Jamadaar. Cursing the Salt Inspector under his breath, Badlu Singh moved towards Alopideen. The Pandit stepped back nervously, “Saheb, for God’s sake, have mercy on me. I am ready to pay twenty-five thousand rupees to settle this issue.”
“Impossible!”
“Thirty thousand then?”
“Not possible at all.”
“Not even forty thousand?”
“Not even forty lakhs!”

”Badlu Singh, take this man into custody. I don’t want to hear another word.” Moral values trampled on Money. Alopideen saw a burly man holding handcuffs advance. He looked around with pleading eyes, then fell unconscious.
The world slept but the news spread. By morning, everyone was commenting on the incident. They all condemned Pandit Alopideen. It seemed as if the world was finally free of sin and its sinners.

The milkman who sold water in the name of milk, the officers who filled daily logs with imaginary entries, the clerks who travelled ticketless on trains, the moneylenders and businessmen who forged documents, all sat in judgement like the Gods.
The next day, handcuffed and flanked by the constables Pandit Alopideen walked towards the courthouse. His heart was filled with contrition and anger and his head hung in shame. There was a buzz in the town. Roofs, balconies and terraces were crammed with spectators.

But the minute he reached the court, people rushed to meet him. Pandit Alopideen was the unchallenged lion of this vast jungle.
The officers of the court were his devotees, the junior staff, his servants, the lawyers, his obedient followers, the orderlies-peons-guards his slaves.
They were surprised not by Alopideen’s act, but about the fact that he got caught by the law. How could the law catch up with a man who had the immense wealth to make anything possible, who could talk himself out of anything? Everyone’s sympathy was with him.

With great alacrity an army of lawyers was deployed to halt this assault on the man of wealth. In the court of law, a battle was being fought between Morality and Money. Munshi Vanshidhar stood in silence. Truth was his strength, a clear statement of facts his only weapon. The witnesses had been swayed by greed.
The Munshi sensed the balance of justice tilt away from him. This was the court of justice but the officers of law seemed to be high on partiality. The trial was over in no time.

In his judgment the Deputy Magistrate pronounced, ‘“The evidence presented against Pandit Alopideen is baseless and misleading. He is a prominent citizen. It is unimaginable that he would stoop for such a small profit. Though the Salt Inspector Munshi Vanshidhar is not much at fault, the court feels grave concern that his brazen and thoughtless act has caused so much trouble to a person of high repute. We are happy that Vanshidhar is sincere and alert in performing his duties, but his excessive loyalty to his job clouded his reason and judgment. He ought to be careful in the future.”

The lawyers heard the verdict and jumped with joy. Pandit Alopideen walked out radiant. His well-wishers showered money over him. An ocean of generosity surged, its waves shook the very foundations of the court.
As Vanshidhar walked out of the courtroom, he was greeted with a spate of taunts. But every mocking voice, every taunt made the pride in his heart glow brighter.

Had Vanshidhar won the case, he probably wouldn’t have strutted out of the courtroom so proud. Today, he realized a regrettable truth about the world. Justice and knowledge, great titles, age and sober demeanour - none of these were worthy of true respect.
Vanshidhar had challenged the might of Big Money, he had to pay its price. Within a few days he received the notice of suspension from the job. Sad and aggrieved, he headed for home.
The old munshi had been grumbling about how his counsel to his son had fallen on deaf ears.

“He does exactly what he wants to do. I am the one who has to answer the wine-seller and the butcher. I am reduced to live a life devoid of any pleasure in my old age. All he gets is the bare salary! I too had a job, a lowly one. But I unabashedly did what needed to be done. My son just wants to flaunt his honesty. The home might wallow in darkness, but he must be seen lighting a lamp at the crossroads. I condemn his understanding of the world. All that education has gone to waste.”
When Vanshidhar reached home crestfallen and told his father of his ordeal, the old man slapped his forehead.

He rubbed his hands in regret, scolded Vanshidhar harshly and if the young man had not removed himself out of his sight, the old munshi might have expressed his anger violently. His mother was sad too. Her wishes to go to the holy towns of Jagannath and Rameshwaram for pilgrimage had turned to dust. Vanshidhar’s wife was so upset she refused to speak to him.
A week passed. The old munshi sat praying on beads in the evening when a grand wagon arrived at their door. It had green and pink curtains, a team of strong bullocks with blue thread strings around their necks and brass cones adorning their horns. The wagon was accompanied by retainers carrying wooden staffs on their shoulders.

The old munshi rushed to greet the guest. It was Pandit Alopideen. The Munshi bowed low and fawned over him with honeyed words. “Fortune has decided to favour us that you have set foot at our doorstep. You are our revered God. But I am not worthy of being in your presence, I have lost face. What to do, my son is a wretched black sheep. One would have been so much better childless than having a son like this.
Alopideen protested, “No no… please brother, don’t say that.”
The old munshi was taken aback, “What else should I call such a son?”

Alopideen replied in a dulcet tone, “How many dutiful men do you find in this world who bring glory to the name of their ancestors and have the ability to sacrifice everything for dharma?”
The pandit then addressed Vanshidhar, “Inspector, please don’t take this as mere flattery. I don’t need to undergo so much trouble to flatter anyone. The other night you arrested me as you had the right and authority to do so. Today, I submit myself to your custody of my own will. I have seen many rich and powerful men, dealt with many senior officers but you are the one who vanquished me. I could enslave everyone else with my money and influence. Please allow me to request something from you.”

Vanshidhar had welcomed Alopideen haughtily. He understood that the gentleman had come to insult and humiliate him further. Vanshidhar did not even try to apologize, he found his father’s flattery unbearable. But as he heard the pandit’s words, the hostility in his heart vanished.
He glanced at Alopideen and saw sincerity in his eyes. His pride now gave way to shame. He spoke hesitantly, “You are being generous. Do forgive me for the unintended incivility. I was shackled by duty otherwise I am your humble servant.”

Alopideen spoke modestly, “You did not accept my request at the river bank, but you have to accept it today”.
“I wouldn’t know if I am worthy of it, but I will try my best to serve you.”
Alopideen took out a stamped letter and placed it in front of Vanshidhar, “Please accept this position and sign this document. I am a Brahmin, and until you fulfil my request I will not budge from your doorstep*.”
As Munshi Vanshidhar read the document his eyes filled with tears of gratitude. Pandit Alopideen had appointed him the permanent manager of his estate at a salary of six thousand rupees per annum along with other daily expenses, a horse to ride, a bungalow to stay in, and servants at no expense to himself.
His voice shook, “Panditji, I am lost for words... I am not worthy of such a high position.”

Alopideen chuckled, “Let us say, I am in need of an unworthy person.”
Vanshidhar replied, “I am always there to serve you. It will be an honour but I have not the education or knowledge required for this role nor do I have the temperament to make up for these shortcomings. You need a highly experienced and farsighted man for such a prestigious position.”
Alopideen took out a pen from the penholder and handed it to Vanshidhar, “I do not desire knowledge or experience or farsightedness, not even efficiency and expertise.”

“I have already had the benefit of these qualities. Now fortune and opportunity have presented me with a pearl which dulls the lustre of experience and knowledge. Please take this pen and sign the document. I pray to God that you remain the same steadfast, fearless, insolent, strong and dutiful Inspector I met at the riverbank the other night.”
Vanshidhar’s eyes welled up. His heart could not contain such generosity. He looked at the pandit with deep respect and with a trembling hand signed the document.
Delighted, Alopideen embraced the young man.
